title: Mud on Wool Carpet
timeEstimate: 30 minutes home, then pro if residual; controlled blot OK
stainChemistry: Mud is mineral particulate (clay + silt + sand) + organic matter (decomposed plant + soil microbes) + water. Sometimes adds tannin (from grass / leaf decay). On wool carpet, the universal-safe trick: LET MUD DRY FIRST. Wet mud spreads with any contact. Dried mud crumbles and vacuums up. The dry-first approach is safer on wool than aggressive wet treatment because wet wool + agitation = felt risk. Wool carpet's banned-tools list: NO enzymes (digest keratin), NO hot water (felts + can't extract), NO alkaline cleaners, NO chlorine bleach. Wool carpet IS more forgiving than wool garments because it doesn't move under treatment, but the felt risk still applies.
whyThisWorks: Letting mud dry completely is the single most important step on wool carpet — wet mud drags into the pile under any contact, dried mud crumbles cleanly. Then vacuum carefully. For residual stain, cold press-blot with wool-safe detergent (no agitation). Pro escalation for set-in mud uses cold extraction with wool-safe NSD. Home protocol: dry-first, vacuum, cold blot, escalate if residual.
defaultAssumption: Wool carpet (knit / woven / Berber). LET MUD DRY first — most home failures on this stain come from rushing wet treatment. After dry, vacuum + cold blot is forgiving. Set-in mud (walked on for weeks, ground in) needs pro extraction.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Let the mud dry completely before any contact. Wet mud + any pressure spreads it through the wool pile. Dry mud crumbles cleanly. This is the universal-safe wool-carpet rule for mud.
Vacuum dried mud thoroughly. Most particulate comes up clean. If a brown stain residue remains under the dried mud, continue.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Mulsolite or wool-safe NSD on damp white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold NSD on a damp white cloth (NEVER pour on wool carpet). Press onto residual stain. No rubbing. Lift, refresh cloth, press again. Wool carpet tolerates controlled blot; never flooding.
If brown tannin residue remains (from grass / leaf decay in mud), pro applies diluted acetic acid via dropper. 60-sec dwell. Flush with cold damp cloth.
Press dry towels on the spot, weight overnight. Fan the area until fully dry. NO heat — even residual heat felts wool fibers in carpet.

steps: 3. Cold wool-safe NSD on damp cloth, press only.
4. Acetic acid spotter for tannin residue (pro), 60-sec ceiling.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — wool carpet felts at 30°C+ when wet
warnings: NEVER touch wet mud — spreads into pile
NEVER hot water — felts wool carpet
NEVER alkaline cleaners — open scales + risk wool damage
NEVER agitation / scrubbing — felts wool fibers

homeSolutions.agent: Wait for Mud to Dry — This Is the Whole Trick
homeSolutions.instruction: The single biggest home mistake on mud + wool carpet is touching wet mud. Wet mud spreads into the pile under any contact — wiping, blotting, even pressing makes the stain bigger. Let it dry completely. A few hours to overnight, depending on the size. Don't walk on it. Don't blot it. Just wait.

Once mud is fully dry and crumbly, vacuum thoroughly with the carpet attachment. Most particulate comes up clean. If you see a brown stain residue UNDER the dried mud, continue. If the carpet looks clean, stop here.

homeSolutions.agent: Wool-Safe Detergent on a DAMP Cloth — Press, Don't Pour
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix half a teaspoon of Eucalan or Soak (or plain clear Dawn) in two cups of COLD water. Dip a clean white cloth, WRING NEARLY DRY, press onto the residual stain. 3 minutes dwell. Lift, refresh cloth, press again. NEVER pour on wool carpet — flooding + agitation = felt risk. Damp-cloth press only. Wool carpet IS more forgiving than wool garments here, but flooding is still a no-go.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, weight with a heavy book, leave overnight. Fan the area in the morning. NO heat — even residual heat from a dryer or hair dryer felts wool fibers in the carpet pile.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro Carpet Cleaner for Set-In Mud — What to Tell Them
homeSolutions.instruction: Take to or call a wool-carpet specialist if mud has been ground in for weeks, if a brown stain ring remains, or if anyone applied hot water / OxiClean before you got there. Tell them: "Mud on wool carpet, age [days / weeks], let dry + vacuum + cold wool-safe detergent press. No hot water, no enzymes, no OxiClean, no scrubbing."

steps: 1. LET MUD DRY COMPLETELY. Don't touch wet mud.
3. Cold wool-safe detergent on a DAMP cloth, press only.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after one careful damp-cloth pass. Repeat home rounds on wool carpet add felt risk.
warnings: NEVER touch wet mud — spreads into pile
NEVER hot water — felts wool carpet
NEVER pour solution on carpet — damp cloth only
NEVER scrub or rub wet wool carpet

neverDo: Never touch wet mud on wool carpet
Never hot water on wool carpet
Never pour cleaning solution on carpet
fiberSensitivities: Wool carpet keratin felts when wet + warm + agitated
Wool carpet tolerates damp-cloth press if cold
Dry-first approach safest on wool carpet for mud
Jute backing common — over-wetting causes browning

materialWarnings: Let mud DRY first — universal-safe rule
Wool carpet is more forgiving than wool garments — but felt risk still applies

escalation.when: Mud has been ground in for weeks, brown stain ring persists after dry-vacuum + cold blot, hot water / OxiClean was applied previously, or wool carpet shows signs of felting.
escalation.whatToTell: "Mud on wool carpet, age [days / weeks], let dry + vacuum + one round cold wool-safe detergent press on damp cloth. No hot water, no enzymes, no OxiClean, no scrubbing. [Residual brown / ring / felting concern]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner specializing in wool carpet (call ahead — confirm cold extraction + wool-safe NSD)

contextQuestions: Fresh wet mud or dried in?
How big — track-in / spill / large area?
Wool carpet type — Berber / cut pile / loop / Oriental rug?
Has anyone touched the wet mud / poured water?
Has hot water or OxiClean been used?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Oriental wool rugs need specialist treatment — different from wall-to-wall wool carpet. Pre-applied hot water = wool may already be partially felted. Anyone touching wet mud likely spread it through the pile — disclose to pro.
